text.title: Red-Green-Refactor with LLM Assistance
text.summary: The three TDD phases adapted for LLM-assisted development, why tests-as-spec save tokens, and common agent failure modes.

section.title: The Three Phases

list.item: RED — Write a failing test that specifies one behavior. Run it. Confirm it fails for the right reason.
GREEN — Write the minimal implementation to make the test pass. No extras, no premature generalization.
REFACTOR — Improve the implementation without changing behavior. Tests must still pass.

section.rule: Never write implementation before a failing test exists. This is the most violated rule in LLM-assisted TDD.
One behavior per RED-GREEN-REFACTOR cycle. Do not batch multiple behaviors into one cycle.

section.title: Why Tests-as-Spec Save Tokens
section.statement: Tests are the most precise specification format for LLMs.
section.rationale: A failing test communicates: the function signature, expected inputs, expected output, and boundary conditions — all in one artifact. An LLM generating implementation from a test needs less clarification than from a prose requirement.

list.item: Prose requirement → test → implementation: 3 steps, 2 ambiguity points
Typical token savings: 20-40% fewer back-and-forth clarification turns

antipattern.description: Agent skips RED step — writes implementation and test together
antipattern.reason: Tests written after implementation confirm the implementation, not the specification. The test may pass but miss edge cases. Enforce RED step with a hook or explicit prompt: "Write a failing test first, do not write implementation yet."

antipattern.description: Agent writes overly broad implementation in GREEN step (premature generalization)
antipattern.reason: GREEN should be the minimal code to pass the current test only. Generalization happens in REFACTOR, driven by the next RED test.
REFACTOR must not change behavior. If new behavior is needed, start a new RED-GREEN cycle.

list.item: Exploratory spikes — spec is unknown until you build it; write tests after exploration is done.
UI layout work — visual output drives design; use screenshots and manual review instead.
Throwaway scripts — not worth the investment.
